title: Extreme heat is gripping New England. An ER doctor shares how to stay safe.
author: Annie Jonas
bullets: New England faces extreme heat, prompting emergency rooms to prepare for heat-related illnesses like dehydration and heat stroke.
Dr. Andrew Eyre highlights symptoms of heat stroke, including confusion and high body temperature, advising immediate cooling measures.
Eyre suggests staying hydrated, using cooling centers, and ensuring vulnerable groups have access to air conditioning during heat waves.

title: Market Basket named a top employer in latest U.S. News & World Report list
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Market Basket named one of U.S. News & World Report's best companies to work for in 2026-2027.
Market Basket's recognition follows a corporate power struggle and the ousting of CEO Arthur T. Demoulas.
U.S. News evaluated 3,900 companies on factors like pay, benefits, stability, and work-life balance.

title: Revolution still chasing permanent move for Matt Turner, possible Designated Player acquisition
author: Hayden Bird
bullets: Chris Tierney named New England Revolution's GM and chief soccer officer, replacing Curt Onalfo, now VP of soccer operations.
Revolution aims to sign goalkeeper Matt Turner permanently; he's on loan from French club Lyon until the end of his term.
Revolution is considering acquiring a Designated Player this summer to strengthen the team with high-caliber talent.

title: One charged with OUI after woman allegedly fell from the roof of a moving car in South Boston
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Brandon Harper, 38, charged with OUI, allegedly drove the car from which a woman fell in South Boston.
The woman was seen sitting and smoking on the car roof before falling; she was taken to Tufts Medical Center.
Harper reportedly showed signs of intoxication, recording a 0.275 blood alcohol level; bail set at $10,000.

title: Boston magazine names Cambridge’s Fallow Kin the Best New Restaurant of 2026
author: Emily Turner
bullets: Fallow Kin in Cambridge named Best New Restaurant of 2026 by Boston magazine's Best of Boston awards.
The restaurant focuses on sustainably sourced, regional ingredients and is led by owners Conor Dennehy and Danielle Ayer.
Fallow Kin praised for its creative vegetable use and received a four-star review from The Boston Globe.

title: Suspect flees after crowd throws drinks at lone BPD officer, prompting renewed staffing worries
author: Ross Cristantiello
bullets: A suspect escaped Boston police after a crowd threw items at an officer during an arrest attempt in Dorchester.
Boston Police Patrolmen’s Association renewed calls for more staffing following the incident, citing officer safety concerns.
Boston City Councilors have been addressing police staffing issues, with resignations rising from 1 in 2018 to 36 in 2022.

title: What you need to know about Boston Harborfest
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: Boston Harborfest runs July 2-4, featuring free family activities, historical reenactments, live music, and fireworks in Boston.
The event encompasses locations such as Downtown Crossing and Christopher Columbus Park, with fireworks visible from various city spots.
The Boston Pops Fireworks Spectacular on July 4 at the Esplanade includes performances by Grammy winners and a drone show.

title: WCVB reshuffles weather lineup with Mike Wankum’s retirement
author: John Waller
bullets: Mike Wankum retires from WCVB, prompting a reshuffle in the weather team lineup.
Cindy Fitzgibbon moves to Wankum's weeknight slot from her weekday morning role.
Kelly Ann Cicalese transitions from weekend to weekday mornings, replacing Fitzgibbon.

title: Boston-area restaurant chain takes Storrowing as opportunity to apologize, and plug their pizza
author: Peter Chianca
bullets: Sal's Pizza truck got stuck under Storrow Drive overpass due to exceeding clearance limits.
Sal's Pizza issued a Facebook apology and used the incident to promote their jumbo pizza slices.
Sal's Pizza, founded in 1990, has about 130 locations in New England, including one in Florida.

title: As heat wave looms, more beaches close due to sewage spill
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: Sewage spills from two sewer main breaks in Haverhill close several North Shore beaches due to unsafe bacteria.
Approximately 8 million gallons of untreated wastewater spill daily into the Merrimack River, affecting beach water quality.
Newburyport Mayor warns of potential public health emergency as heat wave approaches, with repairs ongoing to contain spillage.
